nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers, tributaries, and flooded areas of the Amazon and Orinoco basins; typically found in open water near the surface and mid-water column

decorPreferences: large open swimming areas in the mid-water
robust or artificial plants (fish may uproot delicate plants)
driftwood for cover and territory markers
tightly fitting lid — essential due to powerful jumping behaviour
subdued lighting with floating plants to reduce skittishness

diet.notes: Piscivore in the wild; readily consumes live and frozen foods in captivity including feeder fish, earthworms, river shrimp, large bloodworms, and krill. Adapts to carnivore pellets and sticks over time with patience. Smaller tankmates and fry will be eaten. Avoid a diet of solely feeder fish due to nutritional deficiencies and disease risk.

goodWith: large peaceful tetras kept in shoals
large plecos (as bottom-dwellers they occupy a separate zone)
avoidWith: small fish that will be eaten
aggressive cichlids that may harass shoaling behaviour
conspecifics kept in insufficient numbers (fewer than 5 leads to increased intraspecific aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Chalceus macrolepidotus; shows characteristic large-scale silver body and pink caudal fin. Confirm licensing before use.

summary: A large, active Amazonian characin with a striking silver body, prominent scales, and a vivid pink-to-red caudal fin. An accomplished jumper requiring a tightly sealed tank; best kept in shoals of 5+ with similarly large, robust tankmates. Carnivore — smaller fish will be eaten.
careNotes:
The Pink-Tail Chalceus (Chalceus macrolepidotus) is a large, active schooling characin from the Amazon and Orinoco basins of South America. Adults reach 20–25 cm and are immediately recognisable by their large silver scales and the vivid pink or reddish caudal fin from which they take their common name. The species belongs to the family Chalceidae, elevated from Characidae by Géry & Mahnert — it is not a true characid despite the hobby name "Pinktail Characin."

A spacious tank of at least 300 L with a footprint of 150 cm or more is required to accommodate a shoal of five or more individuals and allow the brisk, mid-water swimming behaviour typical of the species. A tightly fitting, weighted lid is absolutely essential: the Pink-Tail Chalceus is a powerful and frequent jumper and escape losses are common in open-topped or loosely covered tanks. This species should be treated as a high escape risk at all times.

Water should be soft to moderately hard (gH 2–15, kH 1–8) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), reflecting Amazonian conditions. Temperature 24–28 °C suits the species well. Keep the water well-oxygenated with good filtration and regular partial water changes of 25–30% weekly, as the species is sensitive to accumulated waste.

The Pink-Tail Chalceus is a confirmed piscivore and will eat any fish small enough to fit in its mouth. Tank selection must account for adult size: what appears a manageable size difference with juvenile chalceus will narrow rapidly. Large, robust tankmates — sizable cichlids, large barbs, other large tetras kept in shoals — are best suited. Keeping the species in a shoal of five or more reduces intraspecific tension and encourages more natural schooling behaviour; pairs or trios often develop into squabbling hierarchies.

Feeding is straightforward once the fish is settled: frozen krill, cockle, earthworm, and river shrimp are excellent staples. Carnivore pellets and sticks are accepted by most individuals over time. A varied diet prevents nutritional deficiencies. Reliance on live feeder fish should be minimised due to the risk of introducing disease and poor nutritional profile.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is rarely reported and considered extremely difficult. No reliable captive spawning accounts exist in the mainstream aquarium literature. The species is believed to be an egg scatterer in the wild, spawning in open water or among vegetation during seasonal flooding events. Hormonal induction has been used in scientific contexts but is impractical for home aquarists. Treat as not achievable in typical home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Chalceus macrolepidotus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~25 cm), temperature (24–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (Amazon/Orinoco), biotope, diet (piscivore), tank size and shoaling requirements, escape risk notes, and taxonomy including the Chalceidae family elevation from Géry & Mahnert.

additional.name: FishBase — Chalceus macrolepidotus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 25 cm TL), native range countries, taxonomy (Characiformes, Chalceidae), temperature range, and diet classification (carnivore/piscivore). Confirmed family placement as Chalceidae.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Pinktail Chalceus care overview
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed high escape-risk characterisation, piscivore diet, minimum tank size guidance, and shoaling requirement. Noted as a less commonly stocked species in the trade.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Chalceus macrolepidotus species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary UK trade source. Cross-checked adult size, temperament (semi-aggressive, predatory toward smaller fish), water parameters, and tank size recommendations. Confirmed jump risk and need for tight-fitting lid.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Large Amazonian Characins in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Background reference for Amazonian characin husbandry and the family-level taxonomy discussion around Chalceidae. Géry & Mahnert (1991) family elevation noted in the context of larger Characiformes revisions.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive spawning records in the mainstream hobby literature. Believed to scatter eggs in open water or flooded vegetation during seasonal events in the wild. Hormonal induction used in scientific contexts only; not achievable under typical home aquarium conditions.
